subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and gather information to understand a problem that people want to fix by making a new or better tool.

subject: Science
description: Communicate solutions that will reduce the impact of humans on the land, water, air, and/or other living things in the local environment.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas to help reduce how people harm the land, water, air, or living things nearby.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to obtain information about the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for, and respond to, severe weather.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to learn why weather forecasts help people get ready for bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to represent the relationship between the needs of different plants and animals (including humans) and the places they live.
description_plain: Your child can use a model to show how different plants and animals, including people, need certain things and live in certain places.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can explain with examples how plants and animals (including people) change their surroundings to meet their needs.

subject: Science
description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.
description_plain: Your child will watch and share what the weather is like in your area to notice patterns over time.

subject: Science
description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.
description_plain: Your child will watch and describe what plants and animals (including people) need to live and grow.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials provided to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.
description_plain: Your child uses tools and materials to build something that helps keep the Earth cooler by blocking sunlight.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child will observe and describe how sunlight affects the Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to decide if a design works to change how fast or which way an object moves when pushed or pulled.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child will plan and do an experiment to see how different pushes and pulls affect how an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and gather information to understand a problem that people want to fix by making a new or better tool.

subject: Science
description: Make observations at different times of year to relate the amount of daylight to the time of year.
description_plain: Your child can watch how the amount of daylight changes during different times of the year.

subject: Science
description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.
description_plain: Your child can observe the sun, moon, and stars to find patterns that happen again and again.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like, their parents.
description_plain: Your child can observe young plants and animals and explain how they are similar but not exactly like their parents.

subject: Science
description: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behavior of parents and offspring that help offspring survive.
description_plain: Your child can read and watch videos to learn how parents and their babies behave in ways that help the babies survive.

subject: Science
description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can use materials to create a solution to a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.
description_plain: Your child can design and build a tool that uses light or sound to help people communicate from far away.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do simple tests to see what happens when different materials block a beam of light.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects in darkness can be seen only when illuminated.
description_plain: Your child can observe and explain that things in the dark can only be seen when light shines on them.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests of two objects designed to solve the same problem to compare the strengths and weaknesses of how each performs.
description_plain: Your child can look at test results from two objects made to solve the same problem and talk about which one works better and why.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple sketch, drawing, or physical model to illustrate how the shape of an object helps it function as needed to solve a given problem.
description_plain: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions, make observations, and gather information about a situation people want to change to define a simple problem that can be solved through the development of a new or improved object or tool.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and gather information to understand a problem that people want to fix by making a new or better tool.

subject: Science
description: Obtain information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.
description_plain: Your child can find out where water is on Earth and understand that water can be ice or liquid.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to represent the shapes and kinds of land and bodies of water in an area.
description_plain: Your child can create a model that shows the shapes and types of land and water in a certain place.

subject: Science
description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways people try to stop wind or water from changing the land’s shape.

subject: Science
description: Use information from several sources to provide evidence that Earth events can occur quickly or slowly.
description_plain: Your child can use information from different places to show that Earth changes can happen fast or slowly.

subject: Science
description: Make observations of plants and animals to compare the diversity of life in different habitats.
description_plain: Your child can watch plants and animals and compare how many different kinds live in different places.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model that shows how an animal helps spread seeds or pollinate plants.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine if plants need sunlight and water to grow.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see if plants need sunlight and water to grow.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence which changes caused by heating or cooling can be undone and which cannot.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account of how an object made of a small set of pieces can be disassembled and made into a new object.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how an object made of a few parts can be taken apart and made into something new.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data obtained from testing different materials to determine which materials have the properties that are best suited for an intended purpose.
description_plain: Your child can test different materials and decide which ones work best for a certain use.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to describe and sort materials by what they look and feel like.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.
description_plain: Your child comes up with and compares different ideas to lessen how natural Earth events impact people.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and their uses affect the environment.
description_plain: Your child will gather information to explain that energy and fuels come from natural resources and that using them can affect the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth's features.
description_plain: Your child looks at maps and data to describe patterns in Earth’s features.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements to provide evidence of the effects of weathering or the rate of erosion by water, ice, wind, or vegetation.
description_plain: Your child observes or measures how weathering or erosion by water, ice, wind, or plants changes the land.

subject: Science
description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in a landscape over time.
description_plain: Your child uses patterns in rocks and fossils to explain how a landscape has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to describe that animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information in their brain, and respond to the information in different ways.
description_plain: Your child uses a model to show how animals get information through their senses, process it in their brain, and respond in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child explains how plants and animals have body parts inside and outside that help them survive, grow, behave, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Investigate how multiple simple machines work together to perform everyday tasks.
description_plain: Your child explores how simple machines work together to do everyday jobs.

subject: Science
description: Identify types of simple machines and their uses. Investigate and build simple machines to understand how they are used.
description_plain: Your child learns about different simple machines, how they work, and builds some to see how they help.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transfer information.
description_plain: Your child thinks of and compares different ways patterns can be used to send information.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.
description_plain: Your child makes a model to show that we see objects because light reflects off them and enters our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.
description_plain: Your child can build a simple model to show how waves move, including their height and length, and explain how waves can make things move.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.
description_plain: Your child can design, test, and improve a device that changes energy from one type to another.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions and guess what will happen to energy when objects bump into each other.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how energy moves from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electricity.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation relating the speed of an object to the energy of that object.
description_plain: Your child will use evidence to explain how the speed of something relates to its energy.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather-related hazard.
description_plain: Your child can explain why a design helps reduce problems caused by bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe climates in different regions of the world.
description_plain: Your child will gather and combine information to describe different climates around the world.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season.
description_plain: Your child can show weather data in tables and graphs to explain what weather is usually like in a season.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the merit of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and the types of plants and animals that live there may change.
description_plain: Your child can explain if a solution is good for helping plants and animals when their environment changes.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can survive well, some survive less well, and some cannot survive at all.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence why some animals live well in a habitat, some live less well, and some cannot live there at all.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in characteristics among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving, finding mates, and reproducing.
description_plain: Your child understands how differences in animals or plants help them survive, find mates, and have babies.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of the organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils to learn about animals and environments from long ago.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to show how the environment can change traits in living things.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have traits inherited from parents and that variation of these traits exists in a group of similar organisms.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to show that plants and animals get traits from their parents and that these traits can vary.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive.
description_plain: Your child can explain why some animals live in groups to help each other survive.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show that all living things have life stages like birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem that can be solved by applying scientific ideas about magnets.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple problem that can be solved using ideas about magnets.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to determine cause and effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to find out how electric or magnetic forces affect objects without touching.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements of an object's motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.
description_plain: Your child can watch and measure how things move to find patterns that help predict future motion.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to show how balanced and unbalanced forces change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to test and improve a design step-by-step until it works well.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
description_plain: Your child can study test results to find the best parts of different designs and combine them to make a better solution.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions using a systematic process to determine how well they meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can compare different design ideas carefully to see which one best meets the problem's needs and limits.

subject: Science
description: Define the criteria and constraints of a design problem with sufficient precision to ensure a successful solution, taking into account relevant scientific principles and potential impacts on people and the natural environment that may limit possible solutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe the goals and limits of a design problem, considering science and how it affects people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over time.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to understand what has caused global temperatures to rise over the last 100 years.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence how growing human populations and using more resources affect Earth's systems.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use science to design a way to monitor and reduce human harm to the environment.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.
description_plain: Your child will create and use a model to show how uneven heating and Earth's rotation cause wind and ocean currents that affect local climates.

subject: Science
description: Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses results in changes in weather conditions.
description_plain: Your child can gather data to show how moving air masses cause changes in the weather.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to show how water moves through Earth's systems using energy from the sun and gravity.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to support explanations of how natural selection may lead to increases and decreases of specific traits in populations over time.
description_plain: Your child can use math to explain how natural selection can increase or decrease certain traits in a population over time.

subject: Science
description: Gather and synthesize information about the technologies that have changed the way humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.
description_plain: Your child can gather information about how technology has changed the way humans pass on desired traits to organisms.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence that describes how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals' probability of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how differences in genes help some animals survive and have babies better in certain environments.

subject: Science
description: Analyze displays of pictorial data to compare patterns of similarities in the embryological development across multiple species to identify relationships not evident in the fully formed anatomy.
description_plain: Your child can look at pictures of embryos from different species to find similarities that show how they are related.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to construct an explanation for the anatomical similarities and differences among modern organisms and between modern and fossil organisms to infer evolutionary relationships.
description_plain: Your child can explain how similarities and differences in body parts of living and fossil organisms show their evolutionary relationships.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data for patterns in the fossil record that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout the history of life on Earth under the assumption that natural laws operate today as in the past.
description_plain: Your child can study fossil records to find patterns of how life has changed, appeared, or disappeared over Earth's history.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information and sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to explain why asexual reproduction makes identical offspring and sexual reproduction makes varied offspring.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe why structural changes to genes (mutations) located on chromosomes may affect proteins and may result in harmful, beneficial, or neutral effects to the structure and function of the organism.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how changes in genes can affect proteins and cause good, bad, or no effects on an organism.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain how both genes and the environment affect how organisms grow.

subject: Science
description: Use argument based on empirical evidence and scientific reasoning to support an explanation for how characteristic animal behaviors and specialized plant structures affect the probability of successful reproduction of animals and plants, respectively.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to explain how animal behaviors and special plant parts help them reproduce successfully.

subject: Science
description: Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy by chemical processes.
description_plain: Your child can design, build, test, and improve a device that either gives off or takes in heat through chemical reactions.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can create and use a model to show that the number of atoms stays the same during a chemical reaction, so mass doesn’t change.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.
description_plain: Your child can make a model that predicts and explains how particles move, temperature changes, and states of matter change when heat is added or taken away.

subject: Science
description: Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society.
description_plain: Your child can gather and understand information to explain that man-made materials come from natural resources and affect our lives.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.
description_plain: Your child can look at data about substances before and after they mix to decide if a chemical reaction happened.

subject: Science
description: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures.
description_plain: Your child can create models to show what atoms make up simple molecules and larger structures.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that the transfer of thermal energy when two components of different temperature are combined within a closed system results in a more uniform energy distribution among the components in the system (second law of thermodynamics).
description_plain: Your child plans and does an experiment to show how heat moves between things at different temperatures until they even out.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that the transfer of thermal energy when two components of different temperature are combined within a closed system results in a more uniform energy distribution among the components in the system (second law of thermodynamics).
description_plain: Your child plans and does an experiment to show how heat moves between things at different temperatures until they even out.

description_plain: Your child plans and does an experiment to show how heat moves between things at different temperatures until they even out.

description_plain: Your child plans and does an experiment to show how heat moves between things at different temperatures until they even out.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to illustrate that energy at the macroscopic scale can be accounted for as a combination of energy associated with the motions of particles (objects) and energy associated with the relative positions of particles (objects).
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that energy we see comes from both how particles move and how they are positioned.

description: Create a computational model to calculate the change in the energy of one component in a system when the change in energy of the other component(s) and energy flows in and out of the system are known.
description_plain: Your child can create a computer model to calculate how energy changes in one part of a system when other parts gain or lose energy.

description_plain: Your child looks at data to show how volume, pressure, and temperature are connected in gases.

description_plain: Your child can explain what happens in a simple chemical reaction by looking at outer electrons, periodic table trends, and chemical patterns.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.
description_plain: Your child can study data to understand the sizes and distances of objects in our solar system.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how gravity controls the movement of stars, planets, and moons.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to explain the cycles of moon phases, eclipses, and seasons caused by the Earth, sun, and moon.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate competing design solutions for maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem services.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ideas for protecting biodiversity and the benefits ecosystems provide.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by empirical evidence that changes to physical or biological components of an ecosystem affect populations.
description_plain: Your child can argue, using evidence, how changes in an ecosystem affect the populations living there.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of matter and flow of energy among living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child will create a model to show how matter cycles and energy flows between living things and their environment.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interactions among organisms across multiple ecosystems.
description_plain: Your child can explain and predict how different organisms interact in various ecosystems.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child can study data to show how the availability of resources affects animals and plants in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.
description_plain: Your child can explain how photosynthesis helps cycle matter and energy in living things.

subject: Science
description: Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals.
description_plain: Your child can explain why digital signals are better than analog signals for sending information clearly.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how waves can bounce off, be absorbed by, or pass through different materials.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.
description_plain: Your child can use math to describe waves and explain how bigger waves carry more energy.

description: Develop a model to generate data for iterative testing and modification of a proposed object, tool, or process such that an optimal design can be achieved.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to test and improve a design step-by-step until it works well.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data from tests to determine similarities and differences among several design solutions to identify the best characteristics of each that can be combined into a new solution to better meet the criteria for success.
description_plain: Your child can study test results to find the best parts of different designs and combine them to make a better solution.

description_plain: Your child can clearly describe the goals and limits of a design problem, considering science and how it affects people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.
description_plain: Your child can study data about natural disasters to predict future events and help create technology to lessen their impact.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, why Earth's minerals, energy, and water resources are unevenly spread because of past and present Earth processes.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils, rocks, continents, and ocean floors to find proof of how Earth's plates have moved in the past.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how Earth's surface has changed over time due to natural Earth processes.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.
description_plain: Your child will create a model to explain how Earth's materials cycle and how energy moves through this process.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year-old history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how scientists use layers of rock to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year history.

subject: Science
description: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending messages to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.
description_plain: Your child can gather information to show how senses send messages to the brain for quick reactions or memories.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe how food is rearranged through chemical reactions forming new molecules that support growth and/or release energy as this matter moves through an organism.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to explain how food is changed by chemical reactions to help organisms grow or get energy.

subject: Science
description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the body is made of systems that work together, each made of groups of cells.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways parts of cells contribute to the function.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to describe how a cell works and how its parts help it function.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.
description_plain: Your child can do an investigation to show that all living things are made of one or more cells.

subject: Science
description: Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object.
description_plain: Your child can make and share arguments to show that when an object's movement energy changes, energy moves to or from it.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.
description_plain: Your child can plan an experiment to find out how energy transfer relates to the type and amount of matter and temperature changes.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.
description_plain: Your child can design, build, and test a device that either reduces or increases heat transfer.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system.
description_plain: Your child can create a simple model to show how changing the positions of objects that affect each other from a distance changes how much stored energy they have.

subject: Science
description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.
description_plain: Your child can make and understand graphs that show how an object's movement energy changes with its weight and speed.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to show that invisible forces exist between objects even when they are not touching.

subject: Science
description: Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, that gravity pulls objects together and that this pull depends on how heavy the objects are.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions and design a plan to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces.
description_plain: Your child asks questions and plans how to find out what affects the strength of electric and magnetic forces.

subject: Science
description:
Ask questions
  • that arise from careful observation of phenomena, models, or unexpected results, to clarify and/or seek additional information.
  • to identify and/or clarify evidence and/or the premise(s) of an argument.
  • to determine relationships between independent and dependent variables and relationships in models.
  • to clarify and/or refine a model, an explanation, or an engineering problem.
  • that require sufficient and appropriate empirical evidence to answer.
  • that can be investigated within the scope of the classroom, outdoor environment, and museums and other public facilities with available resources and, when appropriate, frame a hypothesis based on observations and scientific principles.
  • that challenge the premise(s) of an argument or the interpretation of a data set.
  • Define a design problem that can be solved through the development of an object, tool, process or system and includes multiple criteria and constraints, including scientific knowledge that may limit possible solutions.

description:
Ask questions
  • that arise from careful observation of phenomena, or unexpected results, to clarify and/or seek additional information
  • that arise from examining models or a theory, to clarify and/or seek additional information and relationships.
  • to determine relationships, including quantitative relationships, between independent and dependent variables.
  • to clarify and refine a model, an explanation, or an engineering problem

description: Each force acts on one particular object and has both strength and a direction. An object at rest typically has multiple forces acting on it, but they add to give zero net force on the object. Forces that do not sum to zero can cause changes in the object's speed or direction of motion. (Boundary: Qualitative and conceptual, but not quantitative addition of forces are used at this level.)
description_plain: Your child understands that forces have strength and direction, and when forces on an object don’t balance, they can change how it moves.

description: Earth's major systems are the geosphere (solid and molten rock, soil, and sediments), the hydrosphere (water and ice), the atmosphere (air), and the biosphere (living things, including humans). These systems interact in multiple ways to affect Earth's surface materials and processes. The ocean supports a variety of ecosystems and organisms, shapes landforms, and influences climate. Winds and clouds in the atmosphere interact with the landforms to determine patterns of weather.

subject: Science
description: Organisms are related in food webs in which some animals eat plants for food and other animals eat the animals that eat plants. Some organisms, such as fungi and bacteria, break down dead organisms (both plants or plants parts and animals) and therefore operate as "decomposers." Decomposition eventually restores (recycles) some materials back to the soil. Organisms can survive only in environments in which their particular needs are met. A healthy ecosystem is one in which multiple species of different types are each able to meet their needs in a relatively stable web of life. Newly introduced species can damage the balance of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals eat plants or other animals in food webs, decomposers break down dead things to recycle materials, and a healthy ecosystem has many species living together in balance.

description: All positions of objects and the directions of forces and motions must be described in an arbitrarily chosen reference frame and arbitrarily chosen units of size. In order to share information with other people, these choices must also be shared. Electric and magnetic (electromagnetic) forces can be attractive or repulsive, and their sizes depend on the magnitudes of the charges, currents, or magnetic strengths involved and on the distances between the interacting objects.
description_plain: Your child knows that to describe where things are and how they move, you need to choose a reference point and units, and share these choices with others; electric and magnetic forces can pull together or push apart depending on charges and distance.

description: Human activities, such as the release of greenhouse gasses from burning fossil fuels, are major factors in the current rise in Earth's mean surface temperature (global warming). Reducing the level of climate change and reducing human vulnerability to whatever climate changes do occur depend on the understanding of climate science, engineering capabilities, and other kinds of knowledge, such as understanding of human behavior and on applying that knowledge wisely in decisions and activities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how human actions like burning fossil fuels cause global warming and why understanding climate science helps us make better choices to reduce its effects.

description: Adaptation by natural selection acting over generations is one important process by which species change over time in response to changes in environmental conditions. Traits that support successful survival and reproduction in the new environment become more common; those that do not become less common. Thus, the distribution of traits in a population changes.
description_plain: Your child understands that species change over time because traits that help them survive and reproduce become more common through natural selection.

subject: Science
description: In sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. Individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. These versions may be identical or may differ from each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that offspring get half their genes from each parent, and these gene versions can be the same or different.

description: In sexually reproducing organisms, each parent contributes half of the genes acquired (at random) by the offspring. Individuals have two of each chromosome and hence two alleles of each gene, one acquired from each parent. These versions may be identical or may differ from each other.
description_plain: Your child understands that offspring get half their genes from each parent, and these gene versions can be the same or different.

description: Variations of inherited traits between parent and offspring arise from genetic differences that result from the subset of chromosomes (and therefore genes) inherited.

subject: Science
description: Genes are located in the chromosomes of cells, with each chromosome pair containing two variants of each of many distinct genes. Each distinct gene chiefly controls the production of specific proteins, which in turn affects the traits of the individual. Changes (mutations) to genes can result in changes to proteins, which can affect the structures and functions of the organism and thereby change traits.
description_plain: Your child understands that genes in chromosomes control traits by making proteins, and changes in genes can change these traits.

subject: Science
description: Food webs are models that demonstrate how matter and energy is transferred between producers, consumers, and decomposers as the three groups interact within an ecosystem. Transfers of matter into and out of the physical environment occur at every level. Decomposers recycle nutrients from dead plant or animal matter back to the soil in terrestrial environments or to the water in aquatic environments. The atoms that make up the organisms in an ecosystem are cycled repeatedly between the living and nonliving parts of the ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child can explain how food webs show the flow of energy and matter between plants, animals, and decomposers in an ecosystem.

description: Similarly, predatory interactions may reduce the number of organisms or eliminate whole populations of organisms. Mutually beneficial interactions, in contrast, may become so interdependent that each organism requires the other for survival. Although the species involved in these competitive, predatory, and mutually beneficial interactions vary across ecosystems, the patterns of interactions of organisms with their environments, both living and nonliving, are shared.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals interact by competing, hunting, or helping each other, and these relationships affect how they live and survive.

subject: Science
description: Energy is a quantitative property of a system that depends on the motion and interactions of matter and radiation within that system. That there is a single quantity called energy is due to the fact that a system's total energy is conserved, even as, within the system, energy is continually transferred from one object to another and between its various possible forms.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy is a measurable property related to how matter and radiation move and interact, and that total energy stays the same even as it changes form.

description_plain: Your child knows that nuclear reactions like fusion, fission, and radioactive decay release or absorb energy, and that the total number of protons and neutrons stays the same during these reactions.

description: Chemical processes, their rates, and whether or not energy is stored or released can be understood in terms of the collisions of molecules and the rearrangements of atoms into new molecules, with consequent changes in the sum of all bond energies in the set of molecules that are matched by changes in kinetic energy.
description_plain: Your child understands that chemical reactions happen because molecules collide and atoms rearrange, which changes energy stored in bonds and affects how fast reactions occur.

description: Stable forms of matter are those in which the electric and magnetic field energy is minimized. A stable molecule has less energy than the same set of atoms separated; one must provide at least this energy in order to take the molecule apart.
description_plain: Your child knows that stable matter has low energy because atoms stick together in molecules that need energy to be broken apart.

description: Current models predict that, although future regional climate changes will be complex and varied, average global temperatures will continue to rise. The outcomes predicted by global climate models strongly depend on the amounts of human-generated greenhouse gasses added to the atmosphere each year and by the ways in which these gasses are absorbed by the ocean and biosphere.
description_plain: Your child understands that global temperatures are expected to keep rising, but regional climate changes will vary depending on how much greenhouse gases humans add and how oceans and life absorb them.

description: The foundation for Earth's global climate systems is the electromagnetic radiation from the sun, as well as its reflection, absorption, storage, and redistribution among the atmosphere, ocean, and land systems, and this energy's re-radiation into space.
description_plain: Your child knows that the sun's energy and how it is absorbed, reflected, and stored by the air, ocean, and land create Earth's climate system.

subject: Science
description: The abundance of liquid water on Earth's surface and its unique combination of physical and chemical properties are central to the planet's dynamics. These properties include water's exceptional capacity to absorb, store, and release large amounts of energy, transmit sunlight, expand upon freezing, dissolve and transport materials, and lower the viscosities and melting points of rocks.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth's water is special because it can store and move energy, let sunlight through, expand when frozen, and help move materials, all of which affect the planet's processes.

description_plain: Your child knows that plate tectonics explains how Earth's surface moves and helps us understand its geologic history.

description: The radioactive decay of unstable isotopes continually generates new energy within Earth's crust and mantle, providing the primary source of the heat that drives mantle convection. Plate tectonics can be viewed as the surface expression of mantle convection.
description_plain: Your child understands that heat from radioactive decay inside Earth drives movements in the mantle, which cause plate tectonics on the surface.

subject: Science
description: The geological record shows that changes to global and regional climate can be caused by interactions among changes in the sun's energy output or Earth's orbit, tectonic events, ocean circulation, volcanic activity, glaciers, vegetation, and human activities. These changes can occur on a variety of time scales from sudden (e.g., volcanic ash clouds) to intermediate (ice ages) to very long-term tectonic cycles.
description_plain: Your child knows that Earth's climate has changed over time because of factors like the sun's energy, Earth's orbit, volcanoes, oceans, glaciers, plants, and human actions.

subject: Science
description: Evidence from deep probes and seismic waves, reconstructions of historical changes in Earth's surface and its magnetic field, and an understanding of physical and chemical processes lead to a model of Earth with a hot but solid inner core, a liquid outer core, a solid mantle and crust. Motions of the mantle and its plates occur primarily through thermal convection, which involves the cycling of matter due to the outward flow of energy from Earth's interior and gravitational movement of denser materials toward the interior.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth has layers including a hot solid inner core, liquid outer core, and solid mantle and crust, and that heat causes the mantle and plates to move.

description: Evidence from deep probes and seismic waves, reconstructions of historical changes in Earth's surface and its magnetic field, and an understanding of physical and chemical processes lead to a model of Earth with a hot but solid inner core, a liquid outer core, a solid mantle and crust. Motions of the mantle and its plates occur primarily through thermal convection, which involves the cycling of matter due to the outward flow of energy from Earth's interior and gravitational movement of denser materials toward the interior.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth has layers including a hot solid inner core, liquid outer core, and solid mantle and crust, and that heat causes the mantle and plates to move.

description: Although active geologic processes, such as plate tectonics and erosion, have destroyed or altered most of the very early rock record on Earth, other objects in the solar system, such as lunar rocks, asteroids, and meteorites, have changed little over billions of years. Studying these objects can provide information about Earth's formation and early history.
description_plain: Your child understands that while Earth's oldest rocks have been changed or destroyed, studying old rocks from the moon and meteorites helps us learn about Earth's early history.

subject: Science
description: Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value.
description_plain: Your child understands that humans rely on nature for resources, but activities like overpopulation and pollution can harm biodiversity, so protecting it helps keep ecosystems and life healthy.

description: Species become extinct because they can no longer survive and reproduce in their altered environment. If members cannot adjust to change that is too fast or drastic, the opportunity for the species' evolution is lost.
description_plain: Your child understands that species go extinct when they can't survive or reproduce in changed environments and can't adapt quickly enough.

subject: Science
description: Natural selection leads to adaptation, that is, to a population dominated by organisms that are anatomically, behaviorally, and physiologically well suited to survive and reproduce in a specific environment. That is, the differential survival and reproduction of organisms in a population that have an advantageous heritable trait leads to an increase in the proportion of individuals in future generations that have the trait and to a decrease in the proportion of individuals that do not.
description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection helps populations become better suited to their environment by increasing traits that help survival and reproduction.

subject: Science
description: Evolution is a consequence of the interaction of four factors: (1) the potential for a species to increase in number, (2) the genetic variation of individuals in a species due to mutation and sexual reproduction, (3) competition for an environment's limited supply of the resources that individuals need in order to survive and reproduce, and (4) the ensuing proliferation of those organisms that are better able to survive and reproduce in that environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that evolution happens because species can grow in number, have genetic differences, compete for resources, and those best suited survive and reproduce more.

description_plain: Your child understands that natural selection happens only when there are genetic differences and trait differences that affect how well individuals do.

subject: Science
description: Genetic information provides evidence of evolution. DNA sequences vary among species, but there are many overlaps; in fact, the ongoing branching that produces multiple lines of descent can be inferred by comparing the DNA sequences of different organisms. Such information is also derivable from the similarities and differences in amino acid sequences and from anatomical and embryological evidence.
description_plain: Your child understands that comparing DNA and physical traits shows how species are related and have evolved over time.

subject: Science
description: In sexual reproduction, chromosomes can sometimes swap sections during the process of meiosis (cell division), thereby creating new genetic combinations and thus more genetic variation. Although DNA replication is tightly regulated and remarkably accurate, errors do occur and result in mutations, which are also a source of genetic variation. Environmental factors can also cause mutations in genes, and viable mutations are inherited.
description_plain: Your child understands that new genetic combinations happen during reproduction and that mutations, sometimes caused by the environment, add to genetic variety.

subject: Science
description: Each chromosome consists of a single very long DNA molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that DNA. The instructions for forming species' characteristics are carried in DNA. All cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. Not all DNA codes for a protein; some segments of DNA are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.
description_plain: Your child understands that DNA in chromosomes carries instructions for traits, and cells use these instructions differently to do their jobs.

subject: Science
description: A complex set of interactions within an ecosystem can keep its numbers and types of organisms relatively constant over long periods of time under stable conditions. If a modest biological or physical disturbance to an ecosystem occurs, it may return to its more or less original status (i.e., the ecosystem is resilient), as opposed to becoming a very different ecosystem. Extreme fluctuations in conditions or the size of any population, however, can challenge the functioning of ecosystems in terms of resources and habitat availability.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems can stay balanced over time and recover from small changes, but big changes can disrupt them.

subject: Science
description: Plants or algae form the lowest level of the food web. At each link upward in a food web, only a small fraction of the matter consumed at the lower level is transferred upward, to produce growth and release energy in cellular respiration at the higher level. Given this inefficiency, there are generally fewer organisms at higher levels of a food web. Some matter reacts to release energy for life functions, some matter is stored in newly made structures, and much is discarded. The chemical elements that make up the molecules of organisms pass through food webs and into and out of the atmosphere and soil, and they are combined and recombined in different ways. At each link in an ecosystem, matter and energy are conserved.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants are the base of food webs, and energy and matter move up the chain but become less available at higher levels.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystems have carrying capacities, which are limits to the numbers of organisms and populations they can support. These limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. Organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. This fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems can only support a limited number of organisms because resources and space are limited.

subject: Science
description: In multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. The organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. Cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.
description_plain: Your child understands that organisms grow by cells dividing and passing on identical genetic information, forming tissues and organs that work together.

subject: Math
description: With guidance, collect data from a simple survey or collaborative investigation; organize data into appropriate single-unit bar graphs, pictographs, and/or tables and draw conclusions based on mathematical observations, comparisons, and grade-level computation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can collect simple data with help, organize it into bar graphs, pictures, or tables, and use it to make basic conclusions.

subject: Math
description: Students collect, organize, and evaluate simple data using grade-level appropriate strategies.
description_plain: Your child can gather, organize, and think about simple data using age-appropriate methods.

subject: Math
description: Identify the value of a penny, nickel, dime, and a collection of pennies, nickels, and dimes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and find the value of pennies, nickels, dimes, and groups of these coins.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest half-hour and relate time to events (before/after, shorter/longer) using analog clocks. Explain how to read hours and minutes using digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest half-hour using analog clocks and explain how to read digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Use direct comparison or a nonstandard unit to compare and order objects according to length, area, capacity, weight, and temperature.
description_plain: Your child can compare and order objects by length, area, capacity, weight, and temperature using direct comparison or simple units.

subject: Math
description: Using standard and non-standard measurements, students compare and order objects, tell time to the hour and half-hour, and investigate beginning concepts of money.
description_plain: Your child can measure and compare objects, tell time to the hour and half-hour, and start learning about money.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal parts; describe the parts using the words halves, fourths, and quarters; and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of, the parts. Understand for partitioning circles and rectangles into two and four equal parts that decomposing into equal parts creates smaller parts.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts, name these parts as halves or quarters, and understand that equal parts are smaller pieces of the whole.

subject: Math
description: Use two-dimensional shapes (e.g., rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (e.g., cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can use flat and solid shapes to make new shapes and combine them in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes of two- and three-dimensional shapes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size). Create and draw two-dimensional shapes with defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell the main features of 2D and 3D shapes, like how many sides or faces they have, and draw shapes based on these features.

subject: Math
description: Students make observations about a shape's defining attributes and utilize them to classify, draw, and compose two-dimensional or three-dimensional shapes. Students begin exploring fractional foundations through the partitioning of rectangles and circles.
description_plain: Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.

subject: Math
description: Create, extend, and give an appropriate rule for number patterns using addition within 100.
description_plain: Your child can make and continue number patterns using addition within 100 and explain the rule for the pattern.

subject: Math
description: Using number sense and place value strategies, add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10. Use models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; describe the strategy and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers within 100, like a two-digit number plus a one-digit number or a multiple of 10, using drawings or place value ideas and explain how they solved it.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems involving addition and subtraction within 20 in situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all parts of the addition or subtraction problem (e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life addition and subtraction problems within 20 by using objects, drawings, or simple equations with unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition facts and the corresponding subtraction facts within 20. Use strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g., 8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4 = 10 + 4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a 10 (e.g., 13 – 4 = 13 – 3 – 1 = 10 – 1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8 + 4 = 12, one knows 12 – 8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13). Model the role of 0 and the equal sign in addition and subtraction using objects or drawings.
description_plain: Your child knows addition and subtraction facts within 20 and uses easy strategies like counting on or making ten to solve problems, showing how zero and the equal sign work.

subject: Math
description: Within the numbers 1-20, students demonstrate fluency and apply addition and subtraction strategies to solve real-world problems. Students apply place value and number sense to add numbers within 100 and investigate beginning algebra concepts through the growing number patterns within 100.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 to solve real problems, add numbers within 100 using place value, and explore simple number patterns.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols > , = , and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at the tens and ones and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Match the ordinal numbers (e.g., first, second, third) with an ordered set of up to 20 items.
description_plain: Your child can match words like first, second, and third to the correct order in a group of up to 20 items.

subject: Math
description: Model place value concepts of two-digit numbers, multiples of 10, and equivalent forms of whole numbers using objects and drawings.
description_plain: Your child can show how two-digit numbers and multiples of 10 are made using objects or drawings to explain place value.

subject: Math
description: Count to at least 120 by ones, fives, and tens from any given number. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count to at least 120 by ones, fives, and tens starting from any number, and can read, write, and show numbers with objects.

subject: Math
description: Students fluently count, read, and represent numbers up to 120 and apply place value concepts to two-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can count, read, and show numbers up to 120 and understands place value for two-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child notices and talks about patterns they see when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child looks for and uses patterns or structures to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses the right tools to help with math tasks.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child explains their thinking about math problems and listens to others to understand their ideas.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child thinks about numbers and quantities in different ways to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child understands math problems and keeps trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: With guidance, collect and organize data into simple bar graphs, pictographs, and/or tables to identify patterns and make comparisons.
description_plain: With help, your child can collect information and organize it into simple charts or graphs to find patterns and compare things.

subject: Math
description: Students begin interacting with data to create and interpret data for patterns and comparison.
description_plain: Your child starts to work with data by creating and understanding simple charts to see patterns and make comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Identify and use appropriate terms to describe intervals of time including: morning, afternoon, evening, today, yesterday, tomorrow, day, week, month, and year; describe how calendars and clocks are tools to measure time.
description_plain: Your child can use words like morning, afternoon, and yesterday to talk about time and understands that calendars and clocks help measure time.

subject: Math
description: Make direct comparisons of the length, capacity, weight, and temperature of objects, and identify which object is shorter, longer, taller, lighter, heavier, warmer, cooler, or holds more.
description_plain: Your child can compare objects to say which is longer, shorter, heavier, lighter, warmer, cooler, or holds more.

subject: Math
description: Students investigate beginning concepts of length, weight, capacity, temperature, and time through observations of direct comparisons.
description_plain: Your child learns about length, weight, capacity, temperature, and time by comparing objects directly.

subject: Math
description: Compare two- and three-dimensional shapes in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/"corners"), and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can compare flat and solid shapes by talking about their size, parts like sides and corners, and other features.

subject: Math
description: Students investigate and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes based on simple attributes.
description_plain: Your child explores and compares different shapes by looking at simple features.

subject: Math
description: Create, extend, and give an appropriate rule for simple repeating and growing patterns with numbers and shapes.
description_plain: Your child can make and continue simple repeating and growing patterns with numbers and shapes and explain the rule.

subject: Math
description: Find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number for any number from 1 to 9 (e.g., by using objects or drawings), and record the answer with a drawing or an equation.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number to make 10 using objects or drawings and show the answer with a picture or equation.

subject: Math
description: Use objects or drawings to model the decomposition of numbers less than 10 into pairs in more than one way. Identify corresponding equations.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers less than 10 in different ways using objects or drawings and write matching equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems that involve addition and subtraction within 10 using modeling with objects or drawings.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction problems within 10 using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Within the numbers 1-10, students use objects and drawings to model the composing (addition) and decomposing (subtraction) of numbers, and solve real-world problems. Students investigate beginning algebra concepts through simple repeating and growing patterns.
description_plain: Your child uses objects and drawings to add and subtract numbers up to 10 and explores simple number patterns.

subject: Math
description: Define and model a "ten" as a group of ten ones. Model equivalent forms of whole numbers from 10 to 20 as groups of tens and ones using objects and drawings.
description_plain: Your child understands that ten ones make a 'ten' and can show numbers from 10 to 20 as groups of tens and ones using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
