description_plain: Your child will trace how the Mongol Empire grew, spread, and influenced other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the institution of slavery in its various forms in Africa, Asia, and the Southwest Pacific, and analyze the impact slavery had on different civilizations.
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of slavery in Africa, Asia, and the Southwest Pacific and explain how slavery affected those societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of Muslim civilization on the growth of cities, the development of trade routes, political organizations, scientific and cultural contributions, and the basis for the early banking system to other cultures of the time. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how Muslim civilization helped cities grow, trade develop, governments form, science and culture advance, and early banking start.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of the Silk Road on the histories of Europe, Africa, and Asia. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe why the Silk Road was important to the histories of Europe, Africa, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the development of sub-Saharan civilizations in Africa and the importance of political and trading centers to the spread of resources, disease, and culture.
description_plain: Your child will study how civilizations in sub-Saharan Africa developed and how political and trade centers helped spread goods, diseases, and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe, compare, and contrast the historical origins, central beliefs, and spread of major religions. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe where major religions began, what they believe, and how they spread.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the conditions that led to the rise of early river valley civilizations and evaluate how the achievements in art, architecture, written language, and religion of those civilizations are related to their respective forms of government and social hierarchies. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands why early river valley civilizations started and how their art, buildings, writing, and religion connect to their governments and social classes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students examine the major movements, events, and figures that contributed to the development of Africa, Asia, and the Southwest Pacific from ancient civilizations to modern times by examining religious institutions, trade and cultural interactions, political institutions, and technological developments.
description_plain: Your child studies important events, people, and ideas that shaped Africa, Asia, and the Southwest Pacific from ancient times to today by looking at religion, trade, politics, and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different ways people save their income, and explain advantages and disadvantages of each.
description_plain: Your child can name different ways people save money and explain the good and bad points of each way.

subject: Social Studies
description: Illustrate how people compare benefits and costs when making choices and decisions as consumers and producers.
description_plain: Your child will show how people think about benefits and costs when they make choices as buyers or sellers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that buyers and sellers interact to determine the prices of goods and services in markets. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that buyers and sellers work together to decide prices for goods and services in markets.

subject: Social Studies
description: List the characteristics of money, and explain how money makes trade and the purchase of goods easier. (E)
description_plain: Your child can list what money is like and explain how money makes trading and buying things easier.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define interdependence, and give examples of how people in the local community depend on each other for goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands that people in a community depend on each other for goods and services and can give examples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of trade in the local community, and explain how trade benefits both parties.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of trade in their community and explain how trade helps both sides.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of goods and services provided by local business and industry. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name goods and services that local businesses and industries provide.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples from the local community that illustrate the scarcity of productive resources. Explain how this scarcity requires people to make choices and incur opportunity costs.
description_plain: Your child understands that resources are limited in their community, so people must make choices and give up some things to get others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain how people in the local community make choices about using goods, services, and productive resources; how they engage in trade to satisfy their economic wants and needs; how they use a variety of resources to gather and apply information about economic changes in the community; and how they compare costs and benefits in economic decision making.
description_plain: Your child learns how people in their community make choices about goods and services, trade to meet needs, use resources to learn about economic changes, and compare costs and benefits when deciding.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how human systems and physical systems have impacted the local environment.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people and natural forces have changed the local environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how Native Americans and early settlers of Indiana adapted to and modified their environment to survive.
description_plain: Your child understands how Native Americans and early settlers in Indiana changed and adapted to their environment to live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the physical characteristics of Indiana to neighboring states using words, illustrations, maps, photographs, and other resources.
description_plain: Your child can compare Indiana’s physical features to nearby states using words, pictures, maps, and photos.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that regions are areas that have similar physical and cultural characteristics. Identify Indiana and the local community as part of a specific region.
description_plain: Your child understands that regions are areas with similar land and culture and can identify Indiana and their community as part of a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the northern, southern, eastern, and western hemispheres; cardinal and intermediate directions; and determine the direction and distance from one place to another on a variety of maps and globes. (E)
description_plain: Your child can find the northern, southern, eastern, and western halves of the Earth, know directions, and figure out how far and which way to go on maps and globes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role citizens have in making decisions and rules within the community, state, and nation, such as participating in local and regional activities, voting in elections, running for office, and voicing opinions in a positive way. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens help make community rules by joining activities, voting, running for office, and sharing opinions respectfully.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of being a responsible citizen of your community, state, and nation. Identify people in your community and state who exhibit the characteristics of good citizenship.
description_plain: Your child knows why it’s important to be a responsible citizen and can identify people in their community and state who show good citizenship.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that the United States has three levels of government (i.e., local, state, and national), and that each level has special duties and responsibilities. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that the United States has local, state, and national governments, each with special jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the duties of and selection process for local and state government officials who make, implement, and enforce laws.
description_plain: Your child can name local and state government officials, explain their duties, and how they are chosen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the reasons governments are needed and identify specific goods and services that governments provide. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands why governments are needed and can name goods and services they provide.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain what it means to be citizens of their community, state, and nation; are able to identify the functions and major services provided by local government; and use a variety of resources to gather information about their local, state, and national governments.
description_plain: Your child learns what it means to be a citizen in their community, state, and nation, knows what local governments do, and uses different resources to learn about government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define immigration and explain how immigration enriches community. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands what immigration means and how new people moving in make communities better.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between fact and fiction in historical accounts by comparing documentary sources on historical figures and events with fictional characters and events in stories. (E)
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between real facts and made-up stories by comparing true historical sources with fictional ones.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of resources to gather information about your region's communities; identify factors that make the region unique, including cultural diversity, industry, the arts, and architecture.
description_plain: Your child uses different resources to learn about their region’s communities and what makes the region special, like its cultures, industries, arts, and buildings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create simple timelines that identify important events in various regions of the state.
description_plain: Your child can make simple timelines showing important events in different parts of the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of people, events, and developments that brought important changes to your community and the region where your community is located. (E)
description_plain: Your child can give examples of people and events that brought important changes to your community and nearby areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of the local community and other communities in the development of the state's regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how your local community and others helped shape the different parts of the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why and how the local community was established, and identify its founders and early settlers.
description_plain: Your child can explain why your community was started and name its founders and first settlers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how Native Americans impacted the development of local communities. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how Native Americans influenced the growth of local communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students describe how significant people, events, and developments have shaped their own community and region; compare their community to other communities in the region in other times and places; and use a variety of resources to gather information about the past.
description_plain: Your child describes important people, events, and changes in their community and compares it to other communities using different sources to learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose and components of a personal budget, and compare factors that influence household saving and spending decisions in early United States history and today.
description_plain: Your child can explain what a personal budget is and compare how families saved and spent money long ago and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Predict the effect of changes in supply and demand on price. (E)
description_plain: Your child can guess how changes in supply and demand affect prices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how education and training, specialization, and investment in capital resources increase productivity.
description_plain: Your child understands how education, special skills, and investing in tools help people work better and produce more.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of technological developments and major inventions on business productivity during the early development of the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe how new inventions and technology helped businesses grow in early America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define types of trade barriers.
description_plain: Your child can name different kinds of trade barriers that affect buying and selling between countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the economic activities within and among Native American cultures prior to contact with Europeans. Examine the economic incentives that helped motivate European exploration and colonization. (E)
description_plain: Your child describes how Native American groups traded before Europeans arrived and why Europeans explored and settled in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students describe the productive resources and market relationships that influence the way people produce goods and services and earn a living in the United States in different historical periods. Students consider the importance of economic decision making and how people make economic choices that influence their future.
description_plain: Your child learns about resources and markets that affect how people make goods, provide services, and earn money in different times in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and analyze how specific physical features influenced historical events. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain how certain land features affected important events in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the ways Native Americans, Africans, immigrant groups, and colonists adapted to variations in the physical environment.
description_plain: Your child describes how Native Americans, Africans, immigrants, and colonists changed how they lived to fit different environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major manufacturing and agricultural regions in colonial America, and summarize the ways that agriculture and manufacturing changed between 1600 and 1800. (E)
description_plain: Your child can find major farming and manufacturing areas in colonial America and explain how these changed between 1600 and 1800.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major sources of accessible fresh water, and describe the impact of access on the local and regional communities.
description_plain: Your child can find main sources of fresh water and explain how having water affected local and regional communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to describe the characteristics of climate regions of the United States.
description_plain: Your child uses maps to describe different climate regions in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate the continental divide and the major drainage basins/watersheds in the United States, noting the watersheds that Indiana lies within.
description_plain: Your child can locate the continental divide and major watersheds in the U.S., including those in Indiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and globes to locate states, capitals, major cities, major rivers, the Great Lakes, and mountain ranges in the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child uses maps and globes to find states, capitals, big cities, major rivers, the Great Lakes, and mountain ranges in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of information resources to identify and evaluate contemporary issues that involve civic responsibility, individual rights, and the common good.
description_plain: Your child uses different sources to find and think about current issues involving rights, responsibilities, and the common good.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine ways by which citizens may effectively voice opinions, monitor government, and bring about change in government, including voting and participation in the election process.
description_plain: Your child learns ways citizens can share their opinions, watch the government, and help make changes, like voting and elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe group and individual actions that illustrate civic virtues, such as civility, cooperation, respect, and responsible participation.
description_plain: Your child can describe actions by groups and individuals that show good citizenship, like respect, cooperation, and responsible participation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the three branches of the United States government, and explain the functions of each. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name the three branches of the U.S. government and explain what each one does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the primary and general election process for local, state, and national offices, including those used to select congressional and presidential office holders.
description_plain: Your child can describe how primary and general elections work for local, state, and national offices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain key ideas about government as noted in the Declaration of Independence, Articles of Confederation, Northwest Ordinance, United States Constitution, and the Bill of Rights. (E)
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain important government ideas from the Declaration of Independence, Articles of Confederation, Northwest Ordinance, Constitution, and Bill of Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain ideas about limited government, the rule of law, and individual rights in key colonial era documents. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands ideas about limited government, rule of law, and individual rights from important colonial documents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the principles and purposes of government as stated in the Preamble to the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can summarize the main goals and principles of government from the Preamble to the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify main components and characteristics of the United States government. Students identify and explain key ideas in government from the colonial and founding periods that continue to shape civic and political life.
description_plain: Your child learns about the parts and features of the U.S. government and important ideas from early American history that still affect how government works today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the contributions of important early American artists and writers and traditional arts and crafts to the new nation's cultural landscape.
description_plain: Your child can identify important early American artists and writers and explain how traditional arts and crafts shaped the nation's culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why the first American political parties developed, and analyze the impact political parties had on early presidential elections.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the first American political parties formed and how they influenced early presidential elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origins and drafting of the Bill of Rights that was ratified in 1791. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how the Bill of Rights was created and approved in 1791.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why the United States Constitution was created in 1787 and how it established a stronger union among the original 13 states by making it the supreme law of the land. Identify people who were involved in its development. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain why the U.S. Constitution was made in 1787, how it made the states stronger together, and who helped create it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain consequences of the American Revolution, including the strengths and weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, changes in trade relationships, and the achievement of independence by the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain what happened after the American Revolution, including the strengths and weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, changes in trade, and winning independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify contributions of women and minorities during the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child can identify how women and minorities contributed during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the contributions of France, other nations, and individuals to the outcome of the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child describes how France, other countries, and individuals helped the American Revolution succeed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major British and American leaders of the American Revolutionary War and describe their significance in key events of the war. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name important British and American leaders in the Revolutionary War and explain their roles in key events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes of the American Revolution as outlined in the Declaration of Independence. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of the American Revolution as stated in the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how political, religious, and economic ideas brought about the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child understands how political, religious, and economic ideas led to the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the early founders of colonial settlements, and describe early colonial resistance to British rule.
description_plain: Your child can name early colonial founders and describe how colonists first resisted British rule.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the 13 British colonies by region (e.g., New England, Middle, Southern). Describe the political and social organization of each region. Explain the economic organization of each region. (E)
description_plain: Your child can find the 13 British colonies on a map by region and explain how each region was organized politically, socially, and economically.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain instances of both cooperation and conflict that existed between Native Americans and colonists.
description_plain: Your child understands examples of both cooperation and conflict between Native Americans and colonists.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the religious, political, and economic reasons for the colonization of the Americas by Europe. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain the different religious, political, and economic reasons why Europeans colonized the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate the early Spanish, French, and British settlements on a map, and compare the origins, physical structure, and social structure of these settlements.
description_plain: Your child can locate early Spanish, French, and British settlements on a map and compare how they started and were organized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast historic Indian groups of the West, Southwest, Northwest, Arctic and sub-Arctic, Great Plains, and Eastern Woodlands regions at the beginning of European exploration in the late fifteenth and sixteenth centuries.
description_plain: Your child can compare different Native American groups from various regions at the time Europeans first explored North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine accounts of early European explorations of North America, including major land and water routes, reasons for exploration, and the impact the exploration had. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe early European explorations of North America, including routes taken, reasons for exploring, and effects of exploration.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe early cultures and settlements that existed in North America prior to contact with Europeans. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands early cultures and settlements in North America before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students describe the historical movements that influenced the development of the United States from pre-Columbian times to 1800, with an emphasis on the American Revolution and the founding of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe important historical events and movements that shaped the United States from before Columbus to 1800, focusing on the American Revolution and founding.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why international trade requires a system for exchanging currency between various countries.
description_plain: Your child can explain why countries need a way to exchange money when they trade with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how countries of Europe and the Americas have been influenced by trade in different historical periods. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how trade has affected countries in Europe and the Americas during different times in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how trade related to key developments in the history of Europe and the Americas. (E)
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how trade influenced important events in the history of Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students examine the influence of physical and cultural factors upon the economic systems of countries in Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child learns how geography and culture affect how countries in Europe and the Americas run their economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare major physical characteristics of regions (biomes) of Europe and the Americas. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare major natural regions (biomes) in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe the climate regions of Europe and the Americas and explain how and why they differ.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe different climate areas in Europe and the Americas and explain why they are different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare major cultural characteristics of regions in Europe and the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare cultural features of regions in Europe and the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate a broad understanding of the countries and capitals of Europe and the Americas. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name many countries and their capitals in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify the characteristics of climate regions in Europe and the Americas, and describe major physical features, countries, and cities of Europe and the Western Hemisphere.
description_plain: Your child learns about climate regions, major land features, countries, and cities in Europe and the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define and compare citizenship and the citizen's role throughout history in Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can explain what citizenship means and how the role of citizens has changed over time in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the impact of major forms of government in Europe and the Americas on civil and human rights.
description_plain: Your child understands how different governments in Europe and the Americas have affected people's civil and human rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the term nation-state and describe the rise of nation-states headed by monarchs in Europe from 1500 to 1700.
description_plain: Your child can explain what a nation-state is and how monarchs led these countries in Europe between 1500 and 1700.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine key ideas of the Magna Carta (1215), the Petition of Right (1628), and the English Bill of Rights (1689) as documents to place limits on the English monarchy and how they have affected the shaping of other governments. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe important documents like the Magna Carta and how they limited the English monarchy and influenced other governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the elements and foundations of Roman representative democracy are present in modern systems of government. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how ideas from Roman representative democracy are used in governments today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the elements and foundations of Greek direct democracy can be found in modern systems of government. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how ideas from Greek direct democracy appear in modern governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast major forms of governments in Europe and the Americas throughout history.
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of governments in Europe and the Americas throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students compare and contrast forms of government in different historical periods with contemporary political structures of Europe and the Americas and examine the rights and responsibilities of individuals in different political systems.
description_plain: Your child learns about different governments from the past and today in Europe and the Americas and understands the rights and duties of people in those systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify issues related to an historical event in Europe or the Americas, giving basic arguments for and against that issue utilizing the perspectives, interests, and values of those involved.
description_plain: Your child can identify issues from a historical event in Europe or the Americas and explain basic arguments for and against using the views of those involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between fact and interpretation in historical accounts and explain the meaning 0of historical passages by identifying who was involved, what happened, where it happened, and relating them to outcomes that followed and gaps in the historical record.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between facts and opinions in history and explain what happened, who was involved, where it happened, and what came next.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define and use the terms decade, century, and millennium, and compare alternative ways that historical periods and eras are designated by identifying the organizing principles upon which each is based.
description_plain: Your child can define time periods like decades, centuries, and millenniums and compare different ways people organize history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the benefits and challenges related to the development of a highly technological society.
description_plain: Your child can talk about the good and bad effects of living in a highly technological society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of industrialization and urbanization on the lives of individuals and on trade and cultural exchange between Europe and the Americas and the rest of the world.
description_plain: Your child can describe how industrialization and city growth changed people's lives and trade between Europe, the Americas, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origins, developments, and innovations of the Industrial Revolution, and explain how the impact of these changes brought about urbanization, changing role of women and child labor. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how the Industrial Revolution started, what new ideas it brought, and how it changed cities, women's roles, and child labor.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the origin and spread of scientific, political, and social ideals associated with the Age of Enlightenment/Age of Reason. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain where and how scientific, political, and social ideas from the Age of Enlightenment began and spread.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Reformations and their effects on European and American society.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Reformations and how they affected society in Europe and America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the interconnections of people, places, and events in the economic, scientific, and cultural exchanges of the European Renaissance that led to the Scientific Revolution, voyages of discovery, and imperial conquest. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people, places, and events during the European Renaissance connected and led to the Scientific Revolution, exploration, and empire building.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the Black Death, along with economic, environmental, and social factors, led to the decline of medieval society. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how the Black Death and other factors caused the decline of medieval society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify trade routes and discuss their impact on the rise of cultural centers and trade cities in Europe and Mesoamerica.
description_plain: Your child can identify trade routes and explain how they helped cities and cultural centers grow in Europe and Mesoamerica.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the diverse points of view and interests of those involved in the Crusades, and give examples of the changes brought about by the Crusades. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain different viewpoints about the Crusades and give examples of changes the Crusades caused.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the development and organization of political, cultural, social, and economic systems in Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how political, cultural, social, and economic systems developed and were organized in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the continuation and contributions of the Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine Empire) after the fall of the Western Roman Empire. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how the Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine Empire) continued and contributed after the Western Roman Empire fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare the beliefs, the spread, and the influence of religions throughout Europe and Mesoamerica.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare how religions spread and influenced people in Europe and Mesoamerica.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the rise, decline, and cultural achievements of ancient civilizations in Europe and Mesoamerica. (E)
description_plain: Your child can tell the story of how ancient civilizations in Europe and Mesoamerica grew, declined, and what they achieved culturally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explore the key historic movements, events, and figures that contributed to the development of modern Europe and America from early civilizations through modern times by examining religious institutions, trade and cultural interactions, political institutions, and technological developments.
description_plain: Your child explores important events, people, and ideas that shaped Europe and America from early times to today by looking at religion, trade, politics, and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify important goods and services provided by state and local governments by giving examples of how state and local tax revenues are used. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name important goods and services that state and local governments provide and explain how tax money pays for them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define profit, and describe how profit is an incentive for entrepreneurs.
description_plain: Your child understands what profit means and how it motivates people to start and run businesses.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify entrepreneurs who have influenced Indiana and the local community.
description_plain: Your child can identify business owners who have made a difference in Indiana and the local community.

subject: Social Studies
description: List the functions of money, and compare and contrast things that have been used as money in the past in Indiana, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can list what money does and compare different things used as money in Indiana, the U.S., and around the world in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that prices change as a result of changes in supply and demand for specific products. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that prices go up or down depending on how much of a product is available and how much people want it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define productivity, and provide examples of how productivity has changed in Indiana during the past century. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows what productivity means and can give examples of how it has changed in Indiana over the last 100 years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the kinds of goods and services produced in Indiana in different historical periods.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of goods and services made in Indiana during different times in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students study and compare the characteristics of Indiana's changing economy in the past and present.
description_plain: Your child studies and compares how Indiana's economy has changed from the past to today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Address misconceptions and misperceptions of Native Americans, Africans, early settlers, and other immigrant groups historically and currently.
description_plain: Your child learns to correct wrong ideas about Native Americans, Africans, early settlers, and immigrant groups from the past and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify immigration patterns into and out of the state, and describe the impact diverse ethnic/native/cultural groups have had and currently have on Indiana.
description_plain: Your child can identify patterns of people moving into and out of Indiana and describe how different cultural groups have influenced the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of major transportation routes in the exploration, settlement, and growth of Indiana and in the state's location as a crossroad of America. (E)
description_plain: Your child explains why major roads and transportation routes helped Indiana grow and become an important crossroads in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the challenges in the physical landscape of Indiana to early settlers and modern day economic development.
description_plain: Your child can name challenges in Indiana’s land that early settlers faced and how these affect the state’s economy today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Indiana's landforms (lithosphere), water features (hydrosphere), and plants and animals (biosphere).
description_plain: Your child describes Indiana’s landforms, water features, plants, and animals.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how glaciers shaped Indiana's landscape and environment. (E)
description_plain: Your child explains how glaciers shaped Indiana’s land and environment long ago.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of resources to take a position or recommend a course of action on a public issue relating to Indiana's past or present.
description_plain: Your child uses different sources to form an opinion or suggest a solution about a public issue in Indiana’s past or present.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how citizens can participate in their state government and explain the right and responsibility of voting.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people take part in state government and understands why voting is both a right and a responsibility.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify major state offices, the duties and powers associated with them, and how they are chosen, such as by election or appointment.
description_plain: Your child can name major state government offices, explain what they do, and how people get these jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the major responsibilities of the legislative (Article 4), executive (Article 5), and judicial branches (Article 7) of state government as written in the Indiana Constitution. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows the main jobs of Indiana’s legislative, executive, and judicial branches as described in the state constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe individual rights, such as freedom of speech and freedom of religion, which people have under Article I of Indiana's Constitution. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands individual rights like freedom of speech and religion that people have under Indiana’s Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major purposes of Indiana's Constitution, as stated in the Preamble. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain the main reasons Indiana’s Constitution was written, as stated in its introduction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students describe the components and characteristics of Indiana's constitutional form of government and explain the levels and three branches of Indiana's government.
description_plain: Your child describes how Indiana’s government is set up, including its levels and three branches.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research and describe the contributions of important Indiana artists and writers to the state's cultural landscape.
description_plain: Your child researches and describes how important Indiana artists and writers have contributed to the state’s culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct a brief narrative about an event in Indiana history using primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child writes a short story about an event in Indiana history using original and secondhand sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and interpret timelines that show relationships among people, events, and movements in the history of Indiana.
description_plain: Your child creates and reads timelines that show how people, events, and movements in Indiana history are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research Indiana's modern growth, emphasizing manufacturing, new technologies, transportation, and global connections.
description_plain: Your child researches how Indiana has grown recently, focusing on factories, new technology, transportation, and global connections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe important events and movements that changed life in Indiana from the mid-twentieth century to the present.
description_plain: Your child identifies and describes important events and changes that affected life in Indiana from the mid-1900s to today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the transformation of Indiana through immigration and developments in agriculture, industry, and transportation.
description_plain: Your child describes how immigration and changes in farming, industry, and transportation transformed Indiana.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe important events and movements that changed life in Indiana in the early twentieth century.
description_plain: Your child identifies and describes important events and changes in Indiana during the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the participation of Indiana citizens in World War I and World War II. (E)
description_plain: Your child describes how people from Indiana took part in World War I and World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of Indiana's increasing agricultural, industrial, political, and business development in the nineteenth century.
description_plain: Your child gives examples of how Indiana’s farming, industry, politics, and business grew in the 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the impact of Abraham Lincoln's presidency on Indiana, and describe the participation of Indiana citizens in the Civil War. (E)
description_plain: Your child summarizes how Abraham Lincoln’s presidency affected Indiana and how Indiana citizens participated in the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the roles of various individuals, groups, and movements in the social conflicts leading to the Civil War. (E)
description_plain: Your child explains the roles of different people and groups in the conflicts that led to the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize and explain the significance of key documents in Indiana's development from a United States territory to statehood.
description_plain: Your child summarizes important documents that helped Indiana become a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the Revolutionary War and other key events and people that influenced the development of Indiana as a state. (E)
description_plain: Your child explains why the Revolutionary War and other key events and people were important for Indiana becoming a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe historic Native American groups that lived in Indiana at the time of early European exploration, including ways these groups adapted to and interacted with the physical environment. (E)
description_plain: Your child identifies Native American groups that lived in Indiana when Europeans first arrived and describes how they lived and used the land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and compare the major early cultures that lived in the region of Indiana before the arrival of the Europeans and before Indiana became a state. (E)
description_plain: Your child identifies and compares the main early cultures that lived in Indiana before Europeans arrived and before it became a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students trace historical periods, places, people, events, and movements that have led to the development of Indiana as a state.
description_plain: Your child traces important times, places, people, events, and movements that helped Indiana become the state it is today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the importance of borrowing and lending (i.e., the use of credit) in the United States economy, and list the advantages and disadvantages of using credit.
description_plain: Your child learns why borrowing and lending money (using credit) is important in the U.S. economy and lists the good and bad sides of using credit.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain and evaluate examples of domestic and international interdependence throughout United States history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the United States has depended on other countries and itself at different times in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and give examples of different kinds of money used in the United States throughout its history.
description_plain: Your child can name and give examples of different types of money used in the U.S. throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Relate how new technology and inventions brought about changes in labor productivity in the United States in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands how new inventions and technology helped workers produce more goods in the 1700s and 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how federal, state, and local governments are involved in the economy of the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain how local, state, and federal governments take part in the U.S. economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify economic factors contributing to European exploration and colonization in North America, the American Revolution, and the drafting of the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can identify economic reasons behind European exploration, the American Revolution, and writing the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify, describe, and evaluate the influence of economic factors on national development from the founding of the nation to the end of Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child understands how economic factors influenced the growth of the United States from its beginning through Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and interpret maps, graphs, and charts showing the distribution of natural resources such as forests, water sources, and wildlife in the United States at the beginning of the nineteenth century. Give examples of how people exploited these resources as the country became more industrialized and people moved westward.
description_plain: Your child can read maps and charts showing natural resources in early 1800s America and explain how people used these resources as the country grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze human and physical factors that have influenced migration and settlement patterns and relate them to the economic development of the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and the land affected where Americans moved and settled, and how this helped the economy grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using primary and secondary sources, identify ways people modified the physical environment as the United States developed, and describe the impacts that resulted.
description_plain: Your child can find examples of how people changed the land as the U.S. developed and describe the effects of those changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using maps, identify changes influenced by growth, economic development, and human migration in the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child can use maps to show how growth, the economy, and migration changed the United States over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the agricultural regions of the United States. Give explanations for how the land was used and developed during the growth of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can name farming regions in the U.S. and explain how the land was used as the country grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major mountain ranges and river systems of the United States and explain the importance of these physical features in the development of America.
description_plain: Your child can name major mountains and rivers in the U.S. and explain why they were important to the country's growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read and interpret maps that portray the physical growth and development of the United States from colonization through Reconstruction (1877). (E)
description_plain: Your child can read maps that show how the U.S. grew and changed from the colonial times through Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify the major geographic characteristics of the United States and its regions. They name and locate the major physical features of the United States, as well as demonstrate a broad understanding of the states, capitals, and major cities, and use geographic skills and technology to examine the influence of geographic factors on national development.
description_plain: Your child can name major U.S. land features, states, capitals, and cities, and use maps and tools to understand how geography affected the country's growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens can monitor and influence the development and implementation of public policies at local, state, and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens can watch and influence government decisions at local, state, and national levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that citizens can participate in the election process (e.g., political parties, campaigns, and elections) at the national, state, and local levels. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people take part in elections through voting, campaigns, and political parties at all government levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of responsible participation by citizens in voluntary civil organizations to bring about social reform.
description_plain: Your child understands why it’s important for citizens to join groups that work to improve society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and explain the relationship between the rights and responsibilities of citizenship in the United States. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands the connection between citizens’ rights and their responsibilities in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the different functions of national and state government within the federal system by analyzing the United States Constitution and the Indiana Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can compare how the national and state governments work by looking at the U.S. and Indiana Constitutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the delegated, reserved, and concurrent powers (division of power or federal system) contained in the United States Constitution. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain the different powers given to the national and state governments by the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain essential ideas of constitutional government, which include limited government; rule of law; due process of law; separated and shared powers; checks and balances; federalism; popular sovereignty; republicanism; representative government; individual rights to life, liberty, and property; and freedom of conscience. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands key ideas of the U.S. Constitution, like limited government, rule of law, shared powers, and individual rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain the major principles, values, and institutions of constitutional government and citizenship, which are based on the founding documents of the United States, and how the three branches of government share and check power within our federal system of government.
description_plain: Your child can explain the main ideas and rules of the U.S. government and how its three branches share and check power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using primary and secondary sources, analyze an issue confronting the United States from colonial times through the Reconstruction period.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to study important issues in U.S. history from colonial times through Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe causes and lasting effects of the Civil War and Reconstruction as well as the political controversies surrounding this time, such as Andrew Johnson's impeachment, the Black Codes, and the Compromise of 1877. (E)
description_plain: Your child can describe what caused the Civil War and Reconstruction, their effects, and political events like Andrew Johnson’s impeachment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the three plans for Reconstruction and evaluate the merits of each. (E)
description_plain: Your child can compare three different plans for Reconstruction and explain the strengths of each.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the factors and individuals which influenced the outcome of the Civil War, and explain the significance of each.
description_plain: Your child can name important people and events that affected the outcome of the Civil War and explain why they mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of events leading to the Civil War, and evaluate the impact issues such as states' rights and slavery had in developing America's sectional conflict. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Civil War and how issues like states’ rights and slavery led to conflict.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of early individual social reformers and movements such as the abolitionist, feminist, and social reform movements.
description_plain: Your child understands how early social reformers and movements, like abolitionists and feminists, influenced America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Abolitionist Movement and identify figures and organizations involved in the debate over slavery, including leaders of the Underground Railroad, and how the movement affected the division between the North and South.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Abolitionist Movement, name key people and groups, and explain how it increased tensions between North and South.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the changing role of women, minorities, and immigrants in the northern, southern, and western parts of the United States in the mid-nineteenth century, and examine possible causes for these changes. (E)
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how women, minorities, and immigrants changed roles in different parts of the U.S. in the mid-1800s and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how immigration affected American culture in the decades before and after the Civil War, including growth of industrial sites in the North; religious differences; tensions between middle-class and working-class people, particularly in the Northeast; and intensification of cultural differences between the North and the South.
description_plain: Your child can explain how immigration affected American culture before and after the Civil War, including industry growth and cultural differences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Mexican War (1846-1848).
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Mexican War and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes, courses, challenges, compromises, and consequences associated with westward expansion, including the concept of Manifest Destiny.
description_plain: Your child can describe why and how Americans moved west, including ideas like Manifest Destiny and the challenges they faced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the key ideas of Jacksonian democracy and explain their influence on political participation, political parties, and constitutional government; analyze Jackson's actions as President, such as the destruction of the National Bank, the nullification crisis, and Jackson's Indian policy. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands key ideas of Jacksonian democracy and how President Jackson’s actions affected politics and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define nationalism and explain how it affected domestic policy, foreign policy, and the development of an industrial economy during this period.
description_plain: Your child can explain what nationalism is and how it influenced U.S. policies and the economy during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of the War of 1812.
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of the War of 1812.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the main issues, consequences, and landmark decisions of the Marshall Court, including how it affected the power of the judicial branch. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain important decisions made by the Marshall Court and how they affected the power of the courts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret how the events surrounding the Louisiana Purchase (1803) and Lewis and Clark expedition (1803-1806) allowed for America's initial push towards westward expansion.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Louisiana Purchase and Lewis and Clark expedition helped America start moving west.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the ways of life in the northern and southern states, including the growth of towns and cities, the growth of industry in the North, and the growing dependence on slavery and the production of cotton in the South, causing early sectionalism in America.
description_plain: Your child can compare life in the North and South, including cities, industry, and slavery, and explain how these differences caused early divisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of important individuals on social and political developments of the time (1775-1800), such as the Independence Movement and the framing of the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important people influenced social and political changes during the American Revolution and the writing of the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the events leading up to the presidential and congressional election of 1800 and the transfer of political authority and power to the Democratic-Republican Party led by Thomas Jefferson (1801); evaluate the significance of these events. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name the events before the 1800 election and explain how Thomas Jefferson's party took power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the views of Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton, and explain how their differences caused the development of political parties, affecting the nation for the future. (E)
description_plain: Your child can tell how Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton had different ideas and how those differences created political parties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the steps taken during the Washington administration and the First and Second Congresses of the United States to establish a stable and lasting national government. (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain the steps George Washington and the first two Congresses took to build a strong national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and provide the significance of major events in the creation of the U.S. Constitution, such as the enactment of state constitutions, the weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, the Constitutional Conventions, the willingness to compromise, and the Federalist vs. anti-Federalist debates regarding the vote to ratify the Constitution. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name important events that led to the U.S. Constitution and explain why they mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Revolutionary War (1775–1783), including the ideas from the Declaration of Independence, the enactment of the Articles of Confederation, and the Treaty of Paris (1783). (E)
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Revolutionary War, what happened during it, and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the reasons and actions for the resistance and rebellion against British imperial rule by the 13 colonies in North America (1761–1775).
description_plain: Your child can explain why the 13 colonies resisted British rule and what actions they took.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions, causes, consequences, and significance of Britain's struggle to maintain control of colonies during the French and Indian War (1754–1763).
description_plain: Your child can explain why Britain fought to keep its colonies during the French and Indian War and what happened as a result.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast reasons for British, French, Spanish, and Dutch colonization in the New World.
description_plain: Your child can compare why the British, French, Spanish, and Dutch came to the New World and how their reasons were different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major Native American groups of eastern North America, and identify cause and effect relationships between European settlers and these Native American groups that led to conflict and cooperation. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name major Native American groups in eastern North America and explain how they sometimes worked with or fought against European settlers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students examine the relationship and significance of themes, concepts, and movements in the development of United States history, including the review of key ideas related to the colonization of America and the revolution and Founding Era. Students also examine the emphasis on social reform, national development and westward expansion, and the Civil War and Reconstruction period.
description_plain: Your child understands important themes and events in U.S. history, like colonization, the Revolution, social changes, westward expansion, the Civil War, and Reconstruction.

subject: Science
description: Plan and carry out fair tests in which variables are controlled and failure points are considered to identify aspects of a model or prototype that can be improved.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do fair tests by controlling variables and thinking about what might go wrong to find ways to improve a model or prototype.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple possible solutions to a problem based on how well each is likely to meet the criteria and constraints of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can come up with and compare several possible solutions to a problem to see which ones best meet the goals and limits.

subject: Science
description: Define a simple design problem reflecting a need or a want that includes specified criteria for success and constraints on materials, time, or cost.
description_plain: Your child can describe a simple design problem based on a need or want, including what success looks like and limits like materials, time, or cost.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect the Earth's resources and environment.
description_plain: Your child can find and combine information about how communities use science to protect Earth's resources and environment.

subject: Science
description: Describe and graph the amounts of salt water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.
description_plain: Your child can describe and make a graph showing how much salt water and fresh water are in different places on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how land, living things, water, and air work together.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
description_plain: Your child can create graphs to show daily changes in shadows, day and night, and how some stars appear differently during seasons.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that the apparent brightness of the sun and stars is due to their relative distances from the Earth.
description_plain: Your child can explain that the sun and stars look brighter or dimmer because of how far away they are from Earth.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how matter moves between plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that plants get the materials they need for growth chiefly from air and water.
description_plain: Your child can explain that plants get most of what they need to grow from air and water.

subject: Science
description: Use models to describe that energy in animals' food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that the energy animals get from food originally comes from the sun.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down.
description_plain: Your child can explain that Earth's gravity pulls objects straight down.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to determine whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.
description_plain: Your child can do an experiment to find out if mixing substances creates new substances.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and measurements to identify materials based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can observe and measure materials to identify them by their properties.

subject: Science
description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total weight of matter is conserved.

description_plain: Your child can spot similarities in computer systems to fix common problems and pick the right hardware and software for tasks.

description_plain: Your child understands that data can be shown in different ways like words, symbols, or colors that people can understand.

description_plain: Your child can show how variables hold and change information in programs.

description_plain: Your child can organize and show data with pictures or charts to explain relationships and support ideas.

description_plain: Your child can break big problems into smaller parts to help solve them.

description: Students select aspects and portions of data to be transformed, clustered, and categorized to provide views and insights about the data.
description_plain: Your child can choose parts of data to group and organize to better understand and explain it.
