subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the causes and effects of changes in the price of certain goods and services has had a significant influence on events in United States history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in prices of goods and services affected important events in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast job skills needed in different time periods in United States history.
description_plain: Your child can compare the job skills people needed during different times in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the importance of borrowing and lending (the use of credit) in the United States economy and list the advantages and disadvantages of using credit.
description_plain: Your child can explain why borrowing and lending money is important in the U.S. economy and list the good and bad sides of using credit.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain and evaluate examples of domestic and international interdependence throughout United States history.
description_plain: Your child can explain examples of how the U.S. has depended on other countries and itself throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Trace the development of the banking system in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the banking system in the U.S. developed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and give examples of different kinds of money used in the United States throughout its history.
description_plain: Your child can name and give examples of different types of money used in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Relate how new technology and inventions brought about changes in labor productivity in the United States in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new inventions changed how work was done in the U.S. during the 1700s and 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze contributions of entrepreneurs and inventors in the development of the United States economy to 1877.
description_plain: Your child can describe how business people and inventors helped grow the U.S. economy before 1877.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how federal, state, and local governments are involved in the economy of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how federal, state, and local governments take part in the U.S. economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the four types of economic systems (traditional, command, market, and mixed); evaluate how the characteristics of a market economy have affected the economic and labor development of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can name four types of economic systems and explain how a market economy shaped the U.S. economy and jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify economic factors contributing to European exploration and colonization in North America, the American Revolution and the drafting of the Constitution of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify economic reasons behind European exploration, the American Revolution, and writing the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify, describe, and evaluate the influence of economic factors on national development from the founding of the nation to the end of Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can describe how economic factors influenced the growth of the U.S. from its start to the end of Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain and give examples of how nature has impacted the physical environment and human populations in specific areas of Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how nature affected the land and people in parts of Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of humans on the physical environment in Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people have changed the land in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the distribution and evaluate the importance of natural resources such as natural gas, oil, forests, uranium, minerals, coal, seafood, and water in Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can compare where natural resources like oil, forests, and water are found in Europe and the Americas and why they are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and interpret maps, graphs and charts showing the distribution of natural resources such as forests, water sources and wildlife in the United States at the beginning of the nineteenth century and give examples of how people exploited these resources as the country became more industrialized and people moved westward.
description_plain: Your child can read maps and charts showing natural resources in early 1800s U.S. and explain how people used these resources as the country grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze human and physical factors that have influenced migration and settlement patterns and relate them to the economic development of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and the environment affected where people moved and settled in the U.S. and how this influenced the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using primary and secondary sources, identify ways people modified the physical environment as the United States developed and describe the impacts that resulted.
description_plain: Your child can use historical sources to show how people changed the land in the U.S. and what effects those changes had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using maps identify changes influenced by growth, economic development, and human migration in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to identify changes caused by growth, economy, and people moving in the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the agricultural regions of the United States and be able to give explanations for how the land was used and developed during the growth of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can name farming areas in the U.S. and explain how the land was used as the country grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ways cultural diffusion, invention, and innovation change culture.
description_plain: Your child can explain how sharing ideas, inventions, and new ways of doing things change cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how ocean currents and winds influence climate differences for Europe and the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ocean currents and winds affect the climate in Europe and the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major mountain ranges and river systems of the United States and explain the importance of these physical features in the development of America.
description_plain: Your child can name major mountains and rivers in the U.S. and explain why they were important for the country's growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the major climate regions in the United States and describe the characteristics of these regions.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe the main climate regions in the U.S. and what makes them different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read and interpret maps that portray the physical growth and development of the United States from colonization through Reconstruction (1877).
description_plain: Your child can read maps that show how the United States grew and changed from the time of colonization through the end of Reconstruction in 1877.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read maps to interpret symbols and determine the landforms and human features that represent physical and cultural characteristics of regions in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can read maps to understand symbols and identify landforms and human-made features that show the physical and cultural parts of different U.S. regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students identify the major geographic characteristics of the United States and its regions. They name and locate the major physical features of the United States, as well as demonstrate a broad understanding of the states, capitals and major cities, and use geographic skills and technology to examine the influence of geographic factors on national development.
description_plain: Your child understands the main geographic features of the U.S. and its regions, can name important physical landmarks, states, capitals, and cities, and uses maps and technology to see how geography influenced the country's growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research and defend positions on issues in which fundamental values and principles related to the United States Constitution are in conflict such as First and Second Amendment rights, the right to privacy, and the rights of the individual.
description_plain: Your child can research and explain different sides of issues where U.S. constitutional rights, like free speech, gun rights, privacy, and individual freedoms, might conflict.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens can monitor and influence the development and implementation of public policies at local, state and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands how citizens can watch and influence government decisions and policies at local, state, and national levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways that citizens can participate in the election process (political parties, campaigns and elections) at the national, state, and local levels.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people take part in elections through voting, political parties, and campaigns at all levels of government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of responsible participation by citizens in voluntary civil organizations to bring about social reform.
description_plain: Your child understands why it’s important for citizens to join volunteer groups to help bring about social change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and explain the relationship between the rights and responsibilities of citizenship in the United States.
description_plain: Your child recognizes that being a citizen means having both rights and responsibilities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the different functions of national and state government within the federal system by analyzing the United States Constitution and the Indiana Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can compare what the national and state governments do by looking at the U.S. and Indiana Constitutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the delegated, reserved, and concurrent powers (division of power or federal system) contained in the United States Constitution.
description_plain: Your child understands the different powers given to the national government, state governments, and those shared by both, as explained in the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine ways that the national government affects the everyday lives of people of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the national government affects people’s daily lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the concept of a separation of powers and how and why these powers are distributed, shared, and limited in the constitutional government of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. government divides power into branches that share, limit, and check each other to keep things balanced.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain essential ideas of constitutional government, which include limited government; rule of law; due process of law; separated and shared powers; checks and balances; federalism; popular sovereignty; republicanism; representative government; and individual rights to life, liberty and property; and freedom of conscience.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain key ideas of the U.S. Constitution like limited government, rule of law, checks and balances, federalism, and individual rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain the major principles, values and institutions of constitutional government and citizenship, which are based on the founding documents of the United States and how the three branches of government share and check power within our federal system of government.
description_plain: Your child understands the main ideas and values of the U.S. government and citizenship, including how the three branches share and check power in our federal system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast examples of art, music, literature, and other forms of expression; explain how these reflect American culture during this time period.
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of art, music, and writing from the time and explain how they show American culture then.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using primary and secondary sources, analyze an issue confronting the United States from colonial times through the Reconstruction period.
description_plain: Your child can use original and later sources to study and analyze important issues the U.S. faced from colonial times through Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between facts and historical interpretations of events, recognizing that the historian's narrative reflects his or her judgment about the significance of particular facts.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between facts and opinions in history and understands that historians choose which facts to focus on.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize historical perspective and evaluate alternative courses of action by describing the historical context in which events unfolded.
description_plain: Your child understands the historical background of events and can think about different choices people could have made at that time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe causes and lasting effects of the Civil War and Reconstruction as well as the political controversies surrounding this time such as Andrew Johnson's impeachment, the Black Codes, and the Compromise of 1877.
description_plain: Your child can describe the causes and lasting effects of the Civil War and Reconstruction, including important political events like Andrew Johnson’s impeachment and the Black Codes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the three plans for Reconstruction and evaluate the merits of each.
description_plain: Your child can compare three different plans for Reconstruction after the Civil War and explain the strengths of each.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the factors and individuals which influenced the outcome of the Civil War and explain the significance of each.
description_plain: Your child can identify important people and factors that influenced the outcome of the Civil War and explain why they mattered.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of events leading to the Civil War, and evaluate the impact issues such as states' rights and slavery had in developing America's sectional conflict.
description_plain: Your child can analyze what caused the Civil War and how issues like states’ rights and slavery led to conflicts between regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of early individual social reformers and movements such as the abolitionist, feminist, and social reform movements.
description_plain: Your child can explain how early social reformers and movements, like abolitionists and feminists, influenced American society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the abolitionist movement and identify figures and organizations involved in the debate over slavery, including leaders of the Underground Railroad, and how the movement affected the division between the North and South.
description_plain: Your child can describe the abolitionist movement, name key people and groups involved, and explain how it increased tensions between the North and South.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of the changing role of women, minorities, and immigrants in the northern, southern and western parts of the United States in the mid-nineteenth century, and examine possible causes for these changes.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how the roles of women, minorities, and immigrants changed in different parts of the U.S. in the mid-1800s and think about why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how immigration affected American culture in the decades before and the Civil War, including growth of industrial sites in the North; religious differences; tensions between middle-class and working-class people, particularly in the Northeast; and intensification of cultural differences between the North and the South.
description_plain: Your child can explain how immigration before the Civil War affected American culture, including industry growth, religious differences, and tensions between social classes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Mexican War (1846-1848).
description_plain: Your child can analyze what caused the Mexican War and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes, courses, challenges, compromises, and consequences associated with westward expansion, including the concept of Manifest Destiny.
description_plain: Your child can describe the causes, events, challenges, compromises, and results of westward expansion, including the idea of Manifest Destiny.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain relationships and conflict between settlers and Native Americans on the frontier.
description_plain: Your child can explain the relationships and conflicts between settlers and Native Americans on the frontier.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the key ideas of Jacksonian democracy and explain their influence on political participation, political parties and constitutional government; analyze Jackson's actions as President such as the destruction of the National Bank, the nullification crisis, and Jackson's Indian policy.
description_plain: Your child can identify key ideas of Jacksonian democracy and explain how Andrew Jackson’s presidency affected politics, including his actions on the National Bank and Native American policy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define nationalism and explain how it affected domestic policy, foreign policy, and the development of an industrial economy during this period.
description_plain: Your child can define nationalism and explain how it influenced U.S. policies and the growth of industry during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of the War of 1812.
description_plain: Your child can analyze the causes and effects of the War of 1812.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the main issues, consequences, and landmark decisions of the Marshall Court, including how it affected the power of the Judicial Branch.
description_plain: Your child can explain important issues and decisions made by the Marshall Court and how they affected the power of the courts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret how the events surrounding the Louisiana Purchase (1803) and Lewis and Clark expedition (1803-1806) allowed for America's initial push towards westward expansion.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Louisiana Purchase and Lewis and Clark expedition helped the U.S. start moving west.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the ways of life in the northern and southern states, including the growth of towns and cities and the growth of industry in the North and the growing dependence on slavery and the production of cotton in the South causing early sectionalism in America.
description_plain: Your child can compare life in the northern and southern states, including city growth and industry in the North and slavery and cotton farming in the South, showing early regional differences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of important individuals on social and political developments of the time (1775-1800) such as the Independence movement and the framing of the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important people influenced social and political changes during the American Revolution and the writing of the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the events leading up to the presidential and congressional election of 1800 and the transfer of political authority and power to the Democratic-Republican Party led by Thomas Jefferson (1801); evaluate the significance of these events.
description_plain: Your child can identify events leading to the 1800 election and explain why the peaceful transfer of power to Thomas Jefferson’s party was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the views of Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton and explain how their differences caused the development of political parties, affecting the nation for the future.
description_plain: Your child can compare the views of Thomas Jefferson and Alexander Hamilton and explain how their disagreements led to the creation of political parties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the steps taken during the Washington Administration and the First and Second Congresses of the United States to establish a stable and lasting national government.
description_plain: Your child can identify steps taken by George Washington and early Congresses to create a strong and lasting national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and provide the significance of major events in the creation of the Constitution such as the enactment of state constitutions, the weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, the Constitutional conventions, the willingness to compromise, and the Federalist- anti-Federalist debates regarding the vote to ratify the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can identify major events in creating the Constitution, including state constitutions, problems with the Articles of Confederation, the Constitutional Convention, compromises made, and debates between Federalists and anti-Federalists.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and effects of the Revolutionary War (1775–1783), including the ideas from the Declaration of Independence, the enactment of the Articles of Confederation, and the Treaty of Paris (1783).
description_plain: Your child can explain what caused the Revolutionary War, what happened because of it, and important documents like the Declaration of Independence, Articles of Confederation, and Treaty of Paris.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain the reasons and actions for the resistance and rebellion against British imperial rule by the thirteen colonies in North America (1761–1775).
description_plain: Your child can name and explain why the thirteen colonies fought against British rule and what actions they took between 1761 and 1775.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the conditions, causes, consequences and significance of Britain's struggle to maintain control of colonies during the French and Indian War (1754–1763).
description_plain: Your child understands why Britain fought to keep control of its colonies during the French and Indian War, what conditions led to the war, and what happened afterward.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast reasons for British, French, Spanish, and Dutch colonization in the New World.
description_plain: Your child can compare why the British, French, Spanish, and Dutch each came to settle in the New World and how their reasons were different or similar.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major Native American Indian groups of eastern North America and identify cause and effect relationships between European settlers and these Native American groups that led to conflict and cooperation.
description_plain: Your child can name major Native American groups in eastern North America and explain how their relationships with European settlers caused both conflicts and cooperation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students examine the relationship and significance of themes, concepts, and movements in the development of United States history, including the review of key ideas related to the colonization of America and the revolution and Founding Era. This will be followed by emphasis on social reform, national development and westward expansion, and the Civil War and Reconstruction period.
description_plain: Your child learns about important events and ideas in early U.S. history, including colonization, the American Revolution, social changes, westward expansion, the Civil War, and Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why people in a community choose different jobs.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people in a community choose different kinds of jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe different kinds of jobs that people do and the tools or equipment used in these jobs. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name different jobs people do and describe the tools or equipment used in those jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that people work to earn money to buy the things they want.
description_plain: Your child understands that people work to earn money so they can buy things they want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain that people do different jobs and work to meet basic economic wants.
description_plain: Your child understands that people have different jobs and work to get the things they need or want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and compare similarities and differences in families, classmates, neighbors and neighborhoods, and ethnic and cultural groups.
description_plain: Your child can find similarities and differences between families, classmates, neighbors, and cultural groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the address and location of the school; understand the importance of an address.
description_plain: Your child can say the school's address, describe where it is, and understand why an address is important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe places in the school and community using words such as here/there, over/under, left/right, above/below, forward/backward, and between.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe places in the school and community using words like here, there, left, right, above, and below.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify maps and globes as ways of representing Earth and understand the basic difference between a map and globe, including the difference in water/oceans and land/continents.
description_plain: Your child knows that maps and globes show the Earth and can explain the difference between them, including how they show land and water.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how to be a responsible family member and member of a group.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how to be a responsible family member and a good member of a group.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of classroom and school rules, and explain the importance of following these rules to ensure order and safety. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name classroom and school rules and explain why following rules helps keep everyone safe and organized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students learn they are citizens of their school, community, and the United States and identify symbols of the nation. They learn the importance of being a responsible citizen who explains why rules are needed and follows them.
description_plain: Your child learns they are part of their school, community, and country, knows important national symbols, and understands why rules are needed and should be followed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that calendars are used to represent the days of the week and months of the year. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that calendars show the days of the week and months of the year.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify events and place events in a sequence. (E)
description_plain: Your child can name events and put them in the order they happened.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways by which people, heritage, and events are commemorated and recognized. (E)
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people, their heritage, and important events are remembered and honored.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare children and families of today with those from the past.
description_plain: Your child can compare how children and families today are similar to or different from those in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students examine the connections of their own environment with the past. They begin to distinguish between events and people of the past and the present and use a sense of time in classroom planning and participation.
description_plain: Your child learns about how their own life connects to the past and can tell the difference between past and present events and people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use information from a variety of sources to describe and discuss current American political issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to describe and talk about current political issues in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use information from a variety of sources to describe and discuss current American political issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to describe and talk about current political issues in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use information from a variety of sources to describe and discuss current American political issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to describe and talk about current political issues in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use information from a variety of sources to describe and discuss current American political issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to describe and talk about current political issues in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe opportunities available to individuals to contribute to the well-being of their communities and participate responsibly in the political process at local, state, and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands ways people can help their communities and take part responsibly in local, state, and national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe opportunities available to individuals to contribute to the well-being of their communities and participate responsibly in the political process at local, state, and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands ways people can help their communities and take part responsibly in local, state, and national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe opportunities available to individuals to contribute to the well-being of their communities and participate responsibly in the political process at local, state, and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands ways people can help their communities and take part responsibly in local, state, and national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe opportunities available to individuals to contribute to the well-being of their communities and participate responsibly in the political process at local, state, and national levels of government.
description_plain: Your child understands ways people can help their communities and take part responsibly in local, state, and national government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens in the United States participate in public elections as voters and supporters of candidates for public office. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows how U.S. citizens vote and support candidates in public elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens in the United States participate in public elections as voters and supporters of candidates for public office. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows how U.S. citizens vote and support candidates in public elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens in the United States participate in public elections as voters and supporters of candidates for public office. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows how U.S. citizens vote and support candidates in public elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how citizens in the United States participate in public elections as voters and supporters of candidates for public office. (E)
description_plain: Your child knows how U.S. citizens vote and support candidates in public elections.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain and give examples of important citizen actions that can impact local, state, and federal government as individuals and members of interest groups.
description_plain: Your child can explain and give examples of how citizens can influence government decisions by acting alone or with groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students explain that because resources are limited, people must make choices, understanding the role that supply, demand, prices, and profits play in a market economy. Key elements of the course include study of scarcity and economic reasoning, supply and demand, market structures, the role of government, national economic performance, the role of financial institutions, economic stabilization, and trade.
description_plain: Your child understands that because resources are limited, people make choices, and they learn how supply, demand, prices, and profits work in the economy.

subject: Math
description: Develop probability models that include the sample space and probabilities of outcomes to represent simple events with equally likely outcomes. Predict the approximate relative frequency of the event based on the model. Compare probabilities from the model to observed frequencies, evaluate the level of agreement, and explain possible sources of discrepancy. (E)
description_plain: Your child can create simple probability models, predict how often events will happen, compare predictions to real results, and explain differences.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Understand that a probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around 1/2 indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event. Understand that a probability of 1 indicates an event certain to occur and a probability of 0 indicates an event impossible to occur. Identify probabilities of events as impossible, unlikely, equally likely, likely, or certain. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is, and can describe events as impossible, unlikely, likely, or certain.

subject: Math
description: Make observations about the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions represented in line plots or box plots. Describe how data, particularly outliers, added to a data set may affect the mean and/or median.
description_plain: Your child can look at graphs of data to see how much two sets overlap and explain how adding unusual data points can change averages.

subject: Math
description: Find, use, and interpret measures of central tendency (mean and median) and measures of spread (range, interquartile range, and mean absolute deviation) for numerical data from random samples to draw comparative inferences about two populations.(E)
description_plain: Your child can find and use averages and measures of spread to compare two groups of data and make conclusions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population. Understand that conclusions and generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population and that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that studying a small, random group can help learn about a larger group if the sample fairly represents it.

subject: Math
description: Students make inferences about populations through sampling and learn about the importance of representative samples.
description_plain: Your child learns how to make guesses about whole groups by studying samples and why samples need to fairly represent the group.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and other mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders and three-dimensional objects composed of right rectangular prisms.(E)
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the volume of cylinders and shapes made from boxes.

subject: Math
description: Understand the formulas for area and circumference of a circle and use them to solve real-world and other mathematical problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses formulas for the area and circumference of circles to solve problems and can explain how they relate.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and other mathematical problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing. Create a scale drawing by using proportional reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, including finding real lengths and areas, and can create scale drawings using proportions.

subject: Math
description: Students use scale drawings, the area and circumference of circles, and the volume of cylinders and other three-dimensional solids to solve real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child uses scale drawings, circle measurements, and volumes of 3D shapes to solve real-life math problems.

subject: Math
description: Graph a line given its slope and a point on the line. Find the slope of a line given its graph.(E)
description_plain: Your child can graph a line when given its slope and a point, and find the slope from a graph.

subject: Math
description: Define slope as vertical change for each unit of horizontal change, and apply that a constant rate of change or constant slope describes a linear function. Identify and describe situations with constant or varying rates of change.
description_plain: Your child understands slope as how much a line goes up or down for each step across and can identify when rates of change are constant or not.

subject: Math
description: Solve inequalities of the form px + q (> or ≥) r or px + q (< or ≤) r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Represent real-world problems using inequalities of these forms and solve such problems. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve and graph inequalities like px + q > r, use them to solve real problems, and explain what the solutions mean.

subject: Math
description: Solve equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r fluently, where p, q , and r are specific rational numbers. Represent real-world problems using equations of these forms and solve such problems.(E)
description_plain: Your child can solve equations like px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, use them to solve real problems, and explain the answers.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems with rational numbers by using one or two operations. (E)
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems using one or two steps with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations (e.g., identity, inverse, commutative, associative, distributive properties) to create equivalent linear expressions, including situations that involve factoring out a common number (e.g., given 2x - 10, create an equivalent expression 2(x - 5)). Justify each step in the process. (E)
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to rewrite expressions in different but equal ways, like turning 2x - 10 into 2(x - 5), and explain why each step works.

subject: Math
description: Students use two variable equations, as well as graphs and tables, to model real-world proportional relationships and connect the constant of proportionality to the idea of slope.
description_plain: Your child will use equations, graphs, and tables with two variables to show real-life proportional relationships and understand how the constant ratio relates to slope.

subject: Math
description: Represent real-world and other mathematical situations that involve proportional relationships. Write equations and draw graphs to represent these proportional relationships. Apply the definition of unit rate to y = mx. (E)
description_plain: Your child can show proportional relationships from real life by writing equations and drawing graphs, and understand unit rates using the formula y = mx.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve ratio and percent problems with multiple operations (e.g., simple interest, tax, markups, markdowns, gratuities, conversions within and across measurement systems, and percent increase and decrease). (E)
description_plain: Your child will solve problems involving ratios and percents, like calculating tax, discounts, interest, and conversions, using proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Identify the unit rate or constant of proportionality in tables, graphs, equations, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate or constant ratio in tables, graphs, equations, or descriptions of proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Students continue to use ratio and rate language, compute using unit rates, and use proportional relationships to solve real-world problems involving ratios and percents.
description_plain: Your child will use ratio and rate language, calculate unit rates, and solve real-world problems involving ratios and percents.

subject: Math
description: Compute fluently with rational numbers using an algorithmic approach. (E)
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately do math operations with fractions, decimals, and negative numbers using step-by-step methods.

subject: Math
description: Apply the inverse relationship between squaring and finding the square root of a perfect square whole number. Find square roots of perfect square whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child will find square roots of perfect squares and understand how squaring and square roots undo each other.

subject: Math
description: Find the prime factorization of whole numbers and write the results using exponents.
description_plain: Your child can break down whole numbers into their prime factors and write these using exponents.

subject: Math
description: Explain that if p and q are integers, then –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q) for all nonzero integers. (E)
description_plain: Your child understands that negative fractions can be written in different but equal ways, like –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q), when p and q are whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. (E)
description_plain: Your child uses math rules, especially the distributive property, to understand why multiplying two negative numbers gives a positive result.

subject: Math
description: Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can find the distance between two numbers on a number line by subtracting and taking the absolute value, and use this idea in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Show on a number line that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Find and interpret sums of rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can show on a number line that a number and its opposite add up to zero and use this idea to solve real-world problems with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Students connect earlier learning to express the prime factorization of whole numbers using exponents, understand the inverse relationship between perfect squares and square roots, and use number lines to compare and order rational and irrational numbers.
description_plain: Your child connects earlier math skills to write prime factorizations with exponents, understand square roots and squares, and use number lines to compare different types of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child looks for and explains patterns that happen again and again when solving math problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child notices and uses the way math problems are organized to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses and uses the right math tools, like rulers or calculators, to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking clearly and understand others’ ideas to find mistakes or better solutions.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child thinks about math problems both in general ideas and with actual numbers to understand and solve them.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child works through math problems carefully and doesn’t give up until they find a solution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the human and physical geographic forces that either bind and unite (centripetal forces) or divide (centrifugal forces) a country or countries. Predict the impact of these forces on the future of these countries, and analyze possible strategies that could be implemented to overcome the impact of centrifugal forces.
description_plain: Your child studies what brings countries together or pulls them apart and thinks about how these forces might affect their future and what can be done about it.
